title_en: Create a Collection-Level Snapshot of Virtual Machines
notes_en: Msvm_CollectionSnapshotService.CreateSnapshot(Collection REF IN, SnapshotSettings string IN, SnapshotType uint16 IN, ResultingSnapshotCollection REF IN/OUT, Job REF OUT). SnapshotType enumeration: 1=Standard Snapshot, 2=Recovery Snapshot. DestroySnapshot(AffectedSnapshotCollection REF) OUT Job.

This feature requires cluster / backup group members that are in an active state (running or saved). A collection-level snapshot produces a group crash-consistent snapshot, and Msvm_VirtualSystemCollection belongs to the Hyper-V Replica and backup group infrastructure (with properties including ReplicationState / ReplicationMode / GroupCrashConsistent / LastApplyVirtualMachineIds). It cannot be produced for test virtual machines that have no operating system and remain stopped: regardless of the collection Type (0/1), SnapshotType (1/2), or whether the member virtual machine has a VHDX attached, CreateSnapshot always returns rv=32770 directly (vendor-specific Failed), producing no asynchronous Job and no readable Msvm_Error object. The related case collection_refpoint falls into the same category.

There is no Msvm_CollectionSnapshotSettingData class, so an empty string can be passed for SnapshotSettings. Although ResultingSnapshotCollection is an IN/OUT REF, passing $null works whether via GetMethodParameters/InvokeMethod or a direct positional call; on success the result should be an Msvm_SnapshotCollection reference.

Collection creation, member addition, and the cleanup chain themselves work (DefineCollection Type0 -> AddMember -> RemoveMember -> DestroyCollection), with each step returning rv=0/4096 normally; only CreateSnapshot fails due to host and guest state constraints. It is therefore recorded as UNSUPPORTED.

Only objects named with WMITEST_ are operated on throughout; cleanup in try/finally removes, in order, the collection snapshot (if any), a fallback pass over per-VM snapshots, RemoveMember, DestroyCollection, DestroySystem, and the vhdx, with no residue confirmed before and after the run.
